annotations.text: What makes this case different from so many others?
The motor weakness restricted to the intrinsic muscles of the left hand could indicate compression of the C8 root or a problem at a higher level in the spinal cord or even the brain.
Presumably, the strength of the muscles innervated by the C5 root, including the deltoid, rhomboid, and biceps muscles, was normal, and no reflexes were noted to be diminished.

That combination of findings is against root involvement at the site of the lesion described on radiographic examination and favors spinal-cord compromise at the C4–5 level as an explanation of the hand weakness.
Distal spread of the biceps reflex (that is, curling of the fingers during testing of the biceps tendon jerk) indicates pathologic exaggeration of this reflex.
This sign also suggests a problem of the pyramidal system above the C6 root level.
The first question raised in the differential diagnosis of post-traumatic pain is whether the trauma itself explains the symptoms and findings.
Fractures of vertebral bodies, subluxations of joints, or acute herniations of disk material can all impinge on nerve roots or the spinal cord.
The radiographs show that this woman's problem could not have been the result of trauma to a previously healthy spine.
Cervical-disk herniations are clearly demonstrated on MRI scanning and do not cause bone destruction.
A fracture with a hematoma can cause a mass compressing the cord, but one would expect to see bone fragments, which were not shown in this case.
The demonstration of erosion of two adjacent vertebral-body end plates with narrowing of the intervening disk space has been considered the radiographic hallmark of infection for over half a century.
The weight of the evidence favors pyogenic infection, and the most likely organism is  *S. aureus*

Several pathological categories, including traumatic, metabolic, congenital, neoplastic, and inflammatory lesions, deserve mention in the differential diagnosis of spinal-cord dysfunction associated with cervical vertebral lesions.
Processes that weaken the spine or compress neural structures can be diffuse or focal, congenital or acquired, neoplastic or inflammatory.
Metabolic disorders such as osteoporosis or osteolysis on the basis of parathyroid disease cause more diffuse involvement than was seen in this case.

However, there is a case report of a health care worker who had a paronychia as a result of a direct inoculation of the finger with tubercle bacilli, and without further details about this patient's occupational exposures as a surgical assistant, tuberculosis remains a theoretical if remote possibility.
I have no way of being sure that the paronychia had any causal relation to the presumptive spinal infection.
In usual clinical practice one might consider an infected nail bed a minor problem.

The authors of a prospective study of solitary bone lesions assessed the value of this blood test and concluded, with a degree of confidence approaching 90 percent, that patients with sedimentation rates above 30 mm per hour had metastatic tumors of unknown origin, marrow-cell tumors, or infections.
the distinction between spinal-root and spinal-cord dysfunction is critical.
It was imperative for her care givers to arrive at an anatomical understanding and diagnosis quickly to preserve the spinal cord.
